data_shape: One markdown file per federal law/Verordnung (~6,000+ acts, repo 451 MB), path pattern <letter>/<slug>/index.md. YAML frontmatter (Title, jurabk, layout, origslug, slug) + pandoc definition-list metadata block carrying amendment provenance in citation form (Ausfertigungsdatum, Fundstelle, 'Neugefasst durch', 'Zuletzt geändert durch: Art. 1 G v. 30.3.2021 I 441', 'textlich nachgewiesen, dokumentarisch noch nicht abschließend bearbeitet' flags). Body: # act title, ##/### Buch/Abschnitt, #### per §, #####/###### deeper units. Generated from gesetze-im-internet.de XML by lawdown.py (gesetze-tools). Known lossy conversion: enumeration numbering dropped (§ 211 StGB Mordmerkmale 1-3 render as unnumbered lines) — markdown alone is NOT faithful to official text structure.
granularity: Act = file; § = addressable via #### markdown heading; Absatz only as textual '(n)' paragraph convention (no structural markup); Satz not marked at all. Numbered enumerations within Absaetze lose their numbers. Fine-grained (Absatz/Satz) node extraction requires your own parsing heuristics and cross-checking against the official XML.
history_support: The headline claim ('git history = amendment archive 2012-2022') is only PARTLY true. Verified structure of the 531-commit history: (a) genuine per-BGBl-issue amendment commits with branch merges named bgbl/YYYY/part-issue, but ONLY Aug 2012 - Jan 2013 (~6 months) — this window is a real per-amendment provenance archive and a good worked example for LexGraph's commit model; (b) total gap 2013-2021; (c) Jan 2021 - Mar 2022: ~7 bulk 'Aktualisierung' commits by outside contributor Daniel Nagy, each touching 73+ laws / ~27k lines, jumping directly from ~2013 state to ~2021/22 state — diffable snapshots but no per-amendment attribution; (d) nothing after 2022-03-26. Net: two snapshot epochs (2012/13 and 2021/22), not continuous history. Checkout-by-date only meaningful inside the 2012/13 window and at the few 2021/22 refresh points. The frontmatter 'geändert durch' citation blocks are independently useful for genesis-chain seeding.
cadence: Dead. Corpus: no data commits since 2022-03-26 (>4 years). gesetze-tools: alive only for dependency/CVE maintenance (last 2026-05-04); the BGBl/gesetze-im-internet scrapers (lawde.py, lawdown.py, LGPL-3.0) still exist and could be re-run to regenerate/extend the corpus yourself. All 166 forks checked: no maintained data successor; closest is Orbiter/bundestag_gesetze_parser with roughly annual solo re-scrapes (2024-05, 2026-03).
licensing: Corpus repo: Unlicense (public-domain dedication, added 2021-03). Underlying statutory texts are amtliche Werke per § 5 UrhG — no copyright. Tools: LGPL-3.0. Access is via GitHub API/raw (GitHub ToS; unauthenticated API limit 60 req/h — clone the repo once instead of API-walking it). No robots.txt/scraping concerns. Cleanest licensing of any German law source.
notes: Use as a HISTORICAL SEED, exactly as hypothesized — but with the corrected expectation that it yields two version checkpoints (Jan-2013 and Mar-2022 states of ~6,000 laws) plus one dense 6-month per-amendment window (Aug 2012 - Jan 2013), not a continuous 2012-2022 archive. Recommended ingestion: git clone once (451 MB), import the 2013 and 2022 tree states as dated snapshots, mine the bgbl/* merge structure as a reference implementation of per-amendment commits, and harvest the frontmatter 'geändert durch' citations for genesis chains. Do NOT rely on the markdown as authoritative text (enumeration numbering lost; 'dokumentarisch noch nicht abschließend bearbeitet' flags present) — pair with gesetze-im-internet.de XML as the current-text source, optionally regenerated via the still-runnable LGPL gesetze-tools scripts. No maintained fork exists to outsource this to.

data_shape: Django-REST JSON, paginated (count/next/previous/results), no auth for read. Cases: metadata + full judgment HTML in detail view (court, date, file_number, type, ecli, slug). Laws: one record per § with full text in gesetze-im-internet DTD-flavored markup + doknr for GII cross-linking. References: pre-resolved edge objects {to, to_hash, law{book,section}, cited_by{kind,id}, marker_text}. Bulk: parquet on HuggingFace (oldp-toolkit generated), plus search endpoints (Elasticsearch-backed) and case_annotations/case_markers for enrichment.
granularity: Laws: per-§ (Einzelnorm) — one API record per paragraph, not per Absatz/Satz. Citation edges: resolved to § level, but marker_text preserves the cited Absatz/Satz string ("§ 167 Abs. 1 Satz 1 VwGO"), so sub-§ targets are recoverable by parsing marker_text. Cases: per decision, with court/date/type metadata; body is unsegmented HTML (Tenor/Gründe headings present).
history_support: Cases: inherently temporal — decisions are dated, immutable events reaching back years; ideal for a time-indexed case graph. Laws: NO true version history — law_books keeps prior scrape snapshots (revision_date, latest=False), e.g. one 2012–2016-era snapshot plus one 2026 snapshot per book, sourced from gesetze-im-internet consolidated text. Cannot checkout norm text by arbitrary date; no amendment-level diffs. The 2022-10-18 HF dump is itself a usable historical snapshot of the corpus.
cadence: Actively maintained in 2026 after a 2022–2026 dump hiatus. Cases ingested within ~1 week of decision (verified: reference to a 2026-06-29 judgment present on 2026-07-06; case decided 2026-05-21 created 2026-05-27). Law books re-scraped ~2026-05-06. HF bulk dumps refreshed 2026-05-22 (previous: 2022-10-18) — assume roughly infrequent/occasional dump refreshes, but API is near-real-time for cases.
licensing: Open. HF datasets: laws-germany and legal-citation-graph-germany under ODbL; legal-citation-graph under CC-BY-4.0; court-decisions-germany untagged, but German court decisions are amtliche Werke (§ 5 UrhG, copyright-free); OLDP platform code is MIT (github.com/openlegaldata/oldp). API is keyless for read; robots.txt explicitly endorses API/dump-based bulk access (Crawl-delay 60 applies to HTML crawling only). ODbL share-alike applies if LexGraph redistributes derived databases of the law/citation-graph dumps.
notes: PRIMARY for its assigned role: the CASE-GRAPH seed. 18.6M pre-resolved case→§ citation edges plus 423,944 full-text decisions with court/date metadata is exactly the interpreted_by/applied_by layer, keyless and fresh (1-week ingest lag verified live). Edges land on § nodes with Abs./Satz recoverable from marker_text — maps cleanly onto LexGraph norm nodes via GII doknr. HOWEVER: honest scoping — the laws component alone would be seed_only for the temporal axis (sparse scrape snapshots of consolidated GII text, no date checkout, no amendment chain); do NOT use OLDP as the norm-version backbone — pair with BGBl/GII-history sources for that. Practical caveats: page_size honored inconsistently (law_books ignored it); ecli frequently empty and some source_url fields are literal "http://example.com", so per-case provenance needs cross-checking against Rechtsprechung-im-Internet/Landesportale; coverage is uneven across courts/Länder (LG/VG present, but no completeness guarantee). Prefer HF parquet dumps (2026-05-20) for the initial bulk load per robots.txt request, then API deltas via created_date/updated_date. ODbL share-alike obligations apply to redistributed derivatives of the ODbL-tagged dumps.

data_shape: One xml.zip per law (6,124 laws), single XML file named by juris doknr (BJNR...), DTD gii-norm.dtd 1.01. Root <dokumente builddate doknr>; first <norm> is the act header (jurabk/amtabk, ausfertigung-datum, fundstelle BGBl periodikum+zitstelle, langue title, standangabe list with standtyp Neuf/Stand and prose standkommentar, act-level fussnoten); then one <norm> per Paragraph with own doknr (BJNE... including an embedded version counter that changes on amendment — cheap diff/change-detection key), metadaten (enbez '§ 1', titel) and textdaten/text/Content with P, DL/DT/DD/LA structured enumerations, BR, pre. Footnotes are semi-structured prose (BVerfG unvereinbar rulings, Hinweise, Textnachweis-ab dates), NOT machine-readable amendment records. HTML/PDF/EPUB renditions also exist per law.
granularity:
Paragraph (§) level: one element per §, with enbez and titel. Absätze appear only as '(1)'-prefixed

children (reliably parseable by regex); Sätze are not marked at all. Enumerations (Nummern/Buchstaben) ARE structured (DL/DT/DD/LA). No per-Absatz or per-Satz identifiers.
history_support: NONE. GII publishes only the current consolidated Fassung; hinweise.html explicitly states Änderungsgesetze are not included, and there is no version archive or Fassung navigation (verified on asylblg/index.html). Amendment history exists only as act-level prose ('zuletzt geändert durch...') and BJNE doknr version counters — predecessors are never published. Historical Fassungen must come from elsewhere (e.g. BGBl archives, juris, or forward snapshotting; community mirror bundestag/gesetze on GitHub has snapshots since ~2013).
cadence: Consolidation is continuous by the Bundesamt für Justiz; each file carries a builddate (AsylbLG rebuilt 2026-06-11 after a 23.4.2026 amendment, i.e. weeks-scale consolidation lag is possible). The Aktualitaetendienst RSS is rebuilt daily (verified lastBuildDate = today) but announces new BGBl I promulgations, not per-law consolidation updates — so change detection for consolidated texts requires re-crawling gii-toc.xml and comparing builddate/BJNE doknr.
licensing: Statutory texts are public domain as amtliche Werke per § 5 Abs. 1 UrhG. Site itself states no license terms; Impressum (HTTP 200) contains only a liability disclaimer; robots.txt permits everything. Non-official additions (nichtamtliche Inhaltsübersichten, editorial footnotes) are flagged 'nichtamtlich' but originate from the same federal documentation and are universally reused (e.g. GitHub bundestag/gesetze). Effectively unrestricted for LexGraph, including commercial use; cite BMJ/BfJ as source.
notes: GII is the canonical, machine-readable source for the CURRENT consolidated state of all ~6,124 federal laws — perfect for seeding LexGraph's HEAD and, if snapshotted daily from now on, for building forward history (builddate + per-norm BJNE version counters make diff detection cheap; full corpus is only ~6k small zips). But it cannot provide back-history: no historical Fassungen, no structured amendment records (change info is prose in standangabe/fussnoten), no effective-date ranges per norm. For checkout-by-date before 'now', pair it with BGBl (recht.bund.de ELI, which the GII RSS already links to) for amendment acts, and consider the bundestag/gesetze GitHub mirror or archive.org captures of GII xml.zips for reconstructed past snapshots. Watch out: latin-1 encoded HTML pages, and consolidation lag of days-to-weeks after BGBl publication (AsylbLG: amended 23.4.2026, file rebuilt 11.6.2026). Update feed is aktuDienst-rss-feed.xml (the intuitive /aktdienst/ path 404s); it signals new BGBl issues, not that a consolidated file was refreshed.

data_shape: Server-rendered HTML only — no API, no JSON, no bulk/dump downloads. But the URL scheme is stable and enumerable: /{para}_{abbrev}.htm (current §), /gesetz/{actId}/index.htm (act), /gesetz/{actId}/l.htm (full change log), /gesetz/{actId}/v{versionId}-YYYY-MM-DD.htm (whole-act synopsis per version date), /gesetz/{actId}/al{changeId}-0.htm (per-§ synopsis), /gesetz/{amendActId}/a{articleId}.htm (amending article). Diff semantics are machine-readable via CSS classes (hdel=deleted, hneu=new, halt=old, hunc=unchanged, hdiff). Amending citations (BGBl part/page/date, article) and Drucksachen references are in structured text near the top of each page. Parsing is straightforward but is HTML scraping, not data access.
granularity: Paragraph (§) is the addressing unit: every § has its own page, its own version chain, and its own dated synopses. Within a synopsis, changes are highlighted at word/sentence level (hdel/hneu spans), and Absatz/Satz structure is preserved in the text, so Absatz/Satz-level diffs are derivable by parsing — but there are no stable Absatz/Satz-level identifiers or URLs. Also provides act-level whole-version synopses and a citation graph (norm-to-norm references, dated).
history_support: Genuinely temporal — the best public one for German federal law: ALL versions of (nearly all) Bundesrecht Teil I since 2006, every version linked to its amending act article, BGBl citation, and (via DIP redirect) Drucksachen/Begruendung — i.e. ready-made commit log, blame, and genesis chain. Hard limitation: nothing before 2006 (older versions must come from BGBl archives / offenegesetze.de / library sources). Future: promulgated changes visible up to 100 days ahead of in-force date.
cadence: Daily ('tagaktuell konsolidiert'); consolidated versions typically live by the day after BGBl promulgation, QA'd against BMJ (gesetze-im-internet) texts. 'Aktuell' (/z.htm) and 'Verkuendet' (/v.htm) pages plus an RSS/Feed and email alert service (Rechtskataster) surface changes as they land.
licensing: No terms of use exist that prohibit scraping — the Impressum (/i.htm) and usage notes (/h.htm) were read in full: no copyright notice, no automated-access clause, no reuse restriction; robots.txt is explicitly permissive for all content pages (only full-text search /s2.htm, /outb/ redirects, and JS are disallowed). HOWEVER: the underlying statutes are public domain (§ 5 UrhG amtliche Werke), but buzer's consolidations, version segmentation, diff alignment and metadata constitute a database protected by the sui generis database right (§§ 87a–87e UrhG). Systematic extraction of substantial parts (i.e. mirroring the version archive) would infringe that right regardless of robots.txt or absent ToS. Polite, low-volume access for QA/cross-checks is defensible; wholesale ingestion for a product requires the operator's permission. The operator is a reachable individual (Daniel Liebig, info@buzer.de) — a cooperation/licensing inquiry is the honest path if deeper use is wanted.
notes: Buzer.de is structurally the closest existing thing to LexGraph itself: per-act git-log (l.htm), per-§ dated synopses with word-level diff markup, amendment-to-BGBl-article provenance, Drucksachen genesis links, dated citation graph, and a 100-day lookahead of pending in-force changes. Everything verified live (11 requests, >=1s apart, all HTTP 200; samples recorded above). It is NOT 'primary' for two reasons: (1) legal — sui generis database right blocks bulk extraction of the editorially-built version archive without an agreement, and there is no API/dump; (2) coverage — history starts at 2006, federal Bundesrecht Teil I only (no Landesrecht, no pre-2006 checkout). Recommended use: (a) gold-standard cross-validation oracle for LexGraph's own consolidation built from primary sources (BGBl/gesetze-im-internet XML + archives), (b) blueprint for data model and diff semantics (hdel/hneu/hunc classes map directly to git-style hunks), (c) seed for the amending-act-to-§ mapping if and only if the operator grants permission — email info@buzer.de before any volume crawling. If permission is refused, downgrade practical use to seed_only/manual-QA. Local copies of all probed pages: /tmp/claude-1000/-home-echo0x22-Desktop-02-PROJECTS-HOBBY-Project-SNTIQ-Gericht/9d449979-121a-494a-9289-3a171a6cd536/scratchpad/ (robots.txt, home.html, i.htm, h.htm, s1_asylblg.html, asylblg.html, p3a.html, l.html, diff.html, vgesamt.html, v.html).

data_shape: Per-Verkündung records. Metadata: RSS 2.0 with rich custom meta: namespace (fundstelle citation, BGBl part/Nr./year, typ, issuing ministry, Ausfertigung date, FNA numbers linking to consolidated-law classification, GESTA ID linking to the Bundestag legislative process) + XML sitemap for full-corpus enumeration + HTML detail pages. Documents: authentic PDF only (regelungstext.pdf + anlageN.pdf, or ZIP bundle). No XML/LegalDocML, no JSON API, no ELI content negotiation.
granularity: Act/promulgation level (one BGBl Nr. = one Verkündung). No paragraph/Absatz/Satz structure — amendment commands are inside the PDF and would need PDF parsing to extract. ELI identifiers are per-issue (eli/bund/bgbl-1/{year}/{nr}), not per-norm-element.
history_support: Append-only permanent archive of all promulgations since 2023-01-01 (2,760 issues verified enumerable) — as a genesis log it IS history and never mutates. But: no pre-2023 BGBl (1949–2022 sits at bgbl.de / Bundesanzeiger Verlag, restricted read-only viewer), and no consolidated/point-in-time norm text at all — this layer records the change events, not the resulting versions.
cadence: Continuous as laws are promulgated (typically several issues per week; feed showed ~500 items over ~8 months). RSS ttl=60min; official guidance: poll RSS or probe next sequential ELI Nr. Newsletter also offered. robots.txt Crawl-delay: 30s — respect it for bulk page crawls; RSS/sitemap polling is low-volume anyway.
licensing: BGBl content is amtliche Werke under § 5 UrhG (public domain / gemeinfrei). Platform is the free-of-charge authentic promulgation medium (BGBl-Modernisierungsgesetz); the official Datenabruf page explicitly documents and endorses automated retrieval. RSS feed itself carries 'Copyright by Bundesministerium der Justiz' boilerplate. Imprint (BMJ, hosted by ITZBund) contains no restrictive reuse terms. Robots.txt permits all content paths used.
notes: This is THE authoritative genesis layer for LexGraph from 2023 onward: legally binding publication, stable ELI identifiers, officially sanctioned programmatic access, and two metadata hooks that wire directly into a temporal graph — GESTA IDs (join to Bundestag DIP/GESTA for the pre-publication legislative chain, i.e. anticipation of pending legislation) and FNA numbers (join to consolidated-law corpora like gesetze-im-internet to know WHICH consolidated norms an issue touches). Ingestion recipe: seed via Sitemap_Verkuendungen.xml (one request = full corpus), then poll rss_bgbl-1-2.xml hourly; fetch ZIP bundles per issue at <=1 req/30s. Limitations to plan around: PDF-only (paragraph-level amendment-command extraction requires PDF parsing — nontrivial but PDFs are born-digital PDF 1.7, not scans), per-issue granularity, nothing before 2023, and no consolidated text (pair with a consolidation source; the NeuRIS project testphase.rechtsinformationen.bund.de reportedly serves LegalDocML XML but was NOT verified in this session). Quirks: HEAD returns 303 (use GET); wrong file extensions are ignored by GSB (a .xml URL happily serves PDF — do not treat 200 as format confirmation); ELI path is case-insensitive for the BGBl-1/bgbl-1 segment.

data_shape: FRBR-modelled (work/expression/manifestation) via the CDM ontology. Metadata: RDF/XML + SPARQL (CSV/JSON). Content: Formex 4 XML (structured, from ~2004 onward, incl. CONSLEG consolidated texts), XHTML, HTML, PDF; delivered by content negotiation on http://publications.europa.eu/resource/celex/{CELEX} (also /resource/eli/... and /resource/cellar/...). Identifiers: CELEX, ELI (http://data.europa.eu/eli/...), CELLAR UUIDs, cross-linked via owl:sameAs. All 24 languages via Accept-Language (ISO 639-3, e.g. 'deu').
granularity: Article and numbered-paragraph (Absatz) level in Formex XML with stable identifiers (<ARTICLE IDENTIFIER="002">, <PARAG IDENTIFIER="002.001">, <ALINEA> for unnumbered subparagraphs). No per-Satz markup. Metadata/ELI granularity is act-level; sub-article addressing must be derived from the Formex structure (deterministic). Pre-2004 acts (incl. 2001/55/EC) exist only as flat HTML/PDF — paragraph structure must be parsed from HTML.
history_support: Real point-in-time support: consolidated versions are first-class dated works (CELEX sector 0, e.g. 02016R0399-20161006/-20170407/-20240710/-20251012, all returned by one SPARQL query and retrievable as dated Formex — verified). Genesis/provenance chains in CDM (based_on_resource_legal, adopts_resource_legal, consolidates, predecessor codifications like 02006R0562-*). Caveats verified live: (1) acts never amended have no consolidation — 2001/55/EC has none, its single 2001 text IS the version history; (2) consolidation is per-language — 02022D0382-20220304 exists only in HUN (language-specific corrigendum), German text of 2022/382 is the unamended base act. Pending legislation (COM proposals, procedure events) also lives in CELLAR sectors 5/PreLex — supports the anticipation feature.
cadence: Official Journal published each working day; CELLAR ingests continuously (same-day for OJ acts). Consolidated versions lag their triggering amendment by ~2-8 weeks (produced by OPOCE, non-authentic). SPARQL/CDM metadata updates daily.
licensing: Excellent. Commission Decision 2011/833/EU (text verified live from CELLAR): documents 'shall be available for reuse' for commercial and non-commercial purposes, free of charge, with source acknowledgment. EUR-Lex/OP legal notice applies CC BY 4.0 to Publications Office material; legal texts themselves carry no copyright restriction on reuse. No API key, no registration for CELLAR REST or SPARQL. Only authentic version is the OJ (consolidations explicitly non-authentic — cite accordingly).
notes: PRIMARY for the EU layer — but only via the CELLAR machine channel, never the website. Key operational facts: (1) eur-lex.europa.eu and op.europa.eu are behind AWS/Azure WAF JS challenges (202/403) — even robots.txt is unreachable; do not scrape the sites. (2) The sanctioned machine route publications.europa.eu/resource/* + /webapi/rdf/sparql is unchallenged, keyless, and returned 200 on every probe. (3) Retrieval recipe: GET /resource/celex/{CELEX} with Accept (application/rdf+xml | text/html | application/xhtml+xml | application/zip;mtype=fmx4 | application/xml;notice=tree) + Accept-Language (ISO 639-3 'deu'); wrong media-type syntax gives explicit 400/404 error messages, easy to handle. (4) For LexGraph temporal semantics: base act = initial commit; each dated CONSLEG (0-prefix CELEX with YYYYMMDD) = snapshot commit; CDM amendment/consolidation relations = edges for diff/blame; per-language consolidation coverage must be modeled (verified HUN-only case). (5) The registered SOAP search webservice and ELI dereference pages exist but were not needed; SOAP requires EU-Login registration and was not probed. (6) For the two target documents: 32001L0055 German full text retrieved (HTML only, pre-Formex era); 32022D0382 German Formex retrieved with Artikel/Absatz identifiers.

data_shape: JSON (or XML via format param). List responses: {numFound, documents[], cursor}. Vorgang: id, titel, vorgangstyp, wahlperiode, beratungsstand, initiative[], sachgebiet[], gesta (GESTA id), zustimmungsbeduerftigkeit[], deskriptor[] (thesaurus terms incl. Rechtsmaterialien type naming affected acts), verkuendung[] {fundstelle 'BGBl I 2026, 197', jahrgang, heftnummer, ausfertigungsdatum, verkuendungsdatum, pdf_url = ELI link to recht.bund.de}, inkrafttreten[] {datum, erlaeuterung per Artikel}, vorgang_verlinkung[] (related Vorgänge), abstract, aktualisiert (ISO timestamp). Vorgangsposition: vorgang_id, datum, vorgangsposition (step name), zuordnung (BT/BR), dokumentart, fundstelle {dokumentnummer, pdf_url}, urheber, gang, fortsetzung. Drucksache: dokumentnummer, drucksachetyp, urheber[], pdf_hash, fundstelle.pdf_url, vorgangsbezug[] backlinks; /drucksache-text and /plenarprotokoll-text add full plaintext. Everything is bidirectionally linked: Vorgang <-> Vorgangsposition <-> Drucksache/Plenarprotokoll.
granularity: Legislative-PROCESS granularity, not norm granularity: entities are Vorgang (procedure), Vorgangsposition (procedural step), Drucksache, Plenarprotokoll, Aktivität, Person. No act/§/Absatz/Satz structure — affected norms appear only as deskriptor names, bill titles, and free text inside Drucksache full text (amendment commands like 'nach § 19 GrEStG ... verlängert' must be parsed from plaintext). Entry-into-force is per-Artikel of the amending law (inkrafttreten[]), not per target norm.
history_support: No norm text versions at all — this source never carries consolidated law text, so it contributes nothing to checkout-by-date of norms. But process history is deep and permanent: Gesetzgebung Vorgänge verified back to Wahlperiode 8 (518 hits, 1976-1980; portal covers documents back further), including failed/lapsed bills ('Für erledigt erklärt'). This makes it the system of record for genesis/provenance chains (Entwurf -> Beratungen -> Beschluss -> BGBl fundstelle) and for anticipation of pending changes, complementing recht.bund.de/gesetze-im-internet which hold the norm texts.
cadence: Continuous/intraday during parliamentary operation — top WP21 record had aktualisiert=2026-07-06T12:01 (same day as probe). Official PDF prescribes delta sync by polling f.aktualisiert.start with overlapping windows (their example polls hourly with 15-min overlap). No rate-limit headers observed; documented courtesy cap: max 25 concurrent requests; further rate-limiting details deliberately not published. GET/HEAD/OPTIONS only.
licensing: Official public API of the German Bundestag; access requires the officially published rotating public API key (currently R2BZaee.DjdCyihKZMf8AOjtScubP2EVydegzjmBIQ, verified live in openapi.yaml and functional 2026-07-06; PDF says the authoritative current key is shown at dip.bundestag.de/über-dip/hilfe/api#content). Terms of use at https://dip.bundestag.de/über-dip/nutzungsbedingungen — page is JS-rendered SPA, text not machine-verifiable here; PDF summarizes: valid key required, read-only use, stability limits. Parliamentary documents are amtliche Werke (§ 5 UrhG context), and the API is explicitly provided for public/machine use; attribute 'Deutscher Bundestag / DIP'. No scraping needed (robots.txt on search.dip.bundestag.de: 404). Build key-rotation handling: re-fetch openapi.yaml periodically since the key rotates (an older key GmEPb1B... appears in the PDF as a stale example).
notes: PRIMARY for the anticipation layer, exactly as scoped — but only for that layer (it is a process graph, not a norm-text source; for temporal checkout it is not even seed_only). Verified capabilities map directly onto LexGraph needs: (1) pending-legislation pipeline: f.vorgangstyp=Gesetzgebung + beratungsstand transitions ('Dem Bundesrat zugeleitet', 'Verkündet', 145/358 WP21 already promulgated) give a state machine for anticipation; (2) genesis chains: /vorgangsposition?f.vorgang={id} reconstructs the full BT/BR Ablauf with document numbers and dserver.bundestag.de PDFs; (3) the promulgation join: verkuendung[].pdf_url is an ELI URL into recht.bund.de (e.g. /eli/bund/BGBl_1/2026/197), giving a clean foreign key from pending Vorgang to the BGBl issue that LexGraph's norm layer ingests — this is the linchpin for closing anticipated diffs into committed history; (4) inkrafttreten[] per-Artikel dates enable scheduling future graph states; (5) /drucksache-text full plaintext of Entwürfe/Beschlussempfehlungen lets you parse amendment commands to pre-compute anticipated diffs; (6) gesta id cross-references the GESTA numbering used elsewhere. Caveats: cursor pagination has no total-page shortcut (iterate until cursor repeats); mapping 'affected norm' requires NLP over titles/deskriptor/full text (no structured norm references); the public key rotates — poll openapi.yaml or the help page and treat 401s as rotation signal. Politeness observed: ~15 requests total, sequential with 1s gaps.

data_shape: REST JSON-LD (schema.org Legislation / DataCatalog, hydra-style pagination) for search + metadata; LegalDocML.de 1.8.2 XML (German Akoma-Ntoso profile) per manifestation with FRBR work/expression/manifestation triple, lifecycle events (ereignis), Geltungszeitgruppen (validity intervals), and stable eIds; also per-document HTML renderings and ZIP bundles (regelungstext + rechtsetzungsdokument + Anlagen + images/PDF). ELI is the primary key: eli/bund/{agent}/{year}/{naturalIdentifier}/{pointInTime}/{version}/{language}[/{pointInTimeManifestation}].
granularity: Document = expression of a work; JSON metadata exposes full article tree (hasPart) with eIds; retrieval per article (§/Art) via {articleEid}.html verified; inside the XML, eIds address down to Absatz, Liste/Listenelement and text nodes (abs-z3, listenelem-n3_text-n1) — Absatz-level anchors are native, Satz-level derivable from the XML structure. Best-in-class granularity among German sources.
history_support: Partial and forward-accruing, not archival. The ELI design is natively temporal ({pointInTime}/{version} = expression) and query params temporalCoverageFrom/To + mostRelevantOn (checkout-by-date) already exist, BUT: (1) the search index holds ~1 expression per work — old-expression metadata 404s; (2) no endpoint enumerates all Fassungen of a work; (3) official docs list 'Versions of norms' as a PLANNED feature; (4) corpus starts from the gesetze-im-internet migration (current consolidations, pointInTime = last amendment) — no back-history to 1949. Crucially, superseded expressions' full LegalDocML ZIPs remain downloadable (verified 200 on a non-indexed expression), and the changelog exposes their URLs, including future in-force expressions (anticipation) — so archiving the changelog from today builds a real version history going forward.
cadence: Continuous updates during Testphase; changelog endpoint accepts arbitrary from/to windows and returned 2448 changed + 117 deleted docs over 10 days, plus allChanged flag for full-resync signaling. Rate limit 600 req/min per IP, no auth. Recommended poll: daily changelog pull + fetch changed ZIPs.
licensing: API explicitly open, no key required (official api-docs). Norm and decision texts are amtliche Werke under § 5 UrhG (public domain by statute); however no explicit open-data license (e.g. DL-DE/BY-2.0) is stated on portal or API docs. Portal code is MIT (github digitalservicebund/ris-search). robots.txt disallows all crawling of the HTML portal — use only the documented API. Testphase disclaimer: operator may change or discontinue the service at any time without notice, and dataset is declared incomplete.
notes: Primary-designate, secondary today. This is the only German source serving consolidated federal norms as LegalDocML.de/Akoma-Ntoso with native temporal ELIs, lifecycle metadata and article/Absatz eIds — architecturally a perfect match for LexGraph's checkout/diff/blame model, and clearly built to become THE canonical temporal source. But honesty requires downgrading it for now: legislation corpus is only 2,414 expressions (~half of in-force federal stock, 0 literature/admin directives), version enumeration per work is not exposed (explicitly 'planned'), old expressions vanish from the index (retrievable only via saved manifestation URLs), bulk dumps are announced but contentUrl:null, and the whole service is a preview that may change without notice. Recommended strategy: (1) start a daily changelog archiver NOW — every changed/deleted manifestation URL + ZIP is a version-history event you cannot recover later; this also captures future in-force expressions (anticipation feature). (2) Use gesetze-im-internet (current text, full corpus) as the seed and BGBl/archives for genesis chains. (3) Re-evaluate for promotion to primary when the versioning endpoint and bulk XML dumps ship. Contact for API feedback: rechtsinformationen@digitalservice.bund.de. Note: one unrelated env issue — the claude.ai Google Calendar MCP connector in this session requires OAuth authorization and is unavailable; irrelevant to this task.

pitfalls: Doc keys != official abbreviations and are not mechanically derivable: AufnG -> BayAsylAufnG (both /Content/Document/AufnG and /BayAufnG verified 404), while DVAsyl -> BayDVAsyl and VwVfG -> BayVwVfG. Build a key map first (POST search, gesabkSuggest, hitlist enumeration, or the 872 links in /Content/Document/ffn).

Full-text search is stateful ASP.NET: GET /Search?SearchFields.Content=... returns 200 but silently NO results; you must GET / for the __RequestVerificationToken + cookie, POST /Search, then read /Search/Hitlist and /Search/Page/<n> with the same cookie jar. /Search/Filter/... GET works without POST but still needs the cookie for paging.

No sitemap.xml (404) and no norms RSS: /Api/Feed covers court decisions only. Change detection for norms = re-parse the ffn Fortführungsnachweis chains or diff the Zip XML / Vollzitat ('zuletzt geändert durch') line.

Zip-XML builddate is regenerated nightly (~02:00) even without content change — do NOT use builddate as a change signal; use <inkraft>, <fassungsdatum>, the aenderungsverlauf elements, or the Vollzitat citation.
Current consolidated version only — no point-in-time archive on the site (XML angaben.versabh carries a single version.id="p"). Historic versions must come from GVBl issues on verkuendung-bayern.de.

XML exists only inside /Content/Zip/<key> (mimetype bayportalnorm+zip); /Content/Xml/ is 404. Format is a proprietary C.H.Beck DTD (byrecht-norm), not LegalDocML — write a dedicated parser; upside: satz.nr sentence numbering and verweis.benorm/eurl/euvo/rspr typed reference targets.
Texts are expressly nichtamtlich (only the paper GVBl is amtlich); Staatskanzlei disclaims Gewähr — keep provenance flags in the graph.

Court decisions: ids are BeckRS-derived (Y-300-Z-BECKRS-B-YYYY-N-NNNNN); redaktionelle Leitsätze/Schlagworte are Beck editorial work NOT free under § 5 UrhG — strip or license-tag them if ingesting Rechtsprechung; Datenbankschutz §§ 87a ff. UrhG is expressly reserved, so avoid marketing the ingest as a wholesale dump of their DB even though the per-document reuse grant is broad.

Be gentle with the ffn page (1.08 MB) and hitlist paging; site sits behind Beck infrastructure with Matomo/Usercentrics — a slow crawler with the contact-UA is fine given Allow: /, but there is no API rate documentation.

pitfalls: GVBl electronic PDFs are 'nachrichtlich' only — the authoritative promulgated GVBl is still the print edition; cite accordingly. BayMBl electronic PDFs ARE the official promulgation.

CSV export silently returns the HTML form (HTTP 200) unless you add export-as=csv to the POST body — the submit-button param alone does nothing; the failure mode is a 200 with text/html instead of text/csv, so check Content-Type.

CSV/Jahreslisten export exists only for BayMBl (volumes 2019-2026, ressort 0-13); there is no GVBl equivalent — for GVBl bulk metadata you must paginate the /gvbl/ search (?offset=N, max itemsPerPage=50, ~2,371 docs 2010-2026).

Document-level data (detail pages, search, HTML full text) starts at 2010 for GVBl; 1945-2009 exists only as issue-level PDFs (older ones scans) plus per-year Jahresinhaltsverzeichnis PDFs — no per-document metadata for that era.

Two different Fundstelle/permalink schemes: GVBl keys documents by first PAGE of the year ('2026 S. 306' -> /gvbl/2026-306/, PDF is per-issue with #page= fragment), BayMBl by running document NUMBER ('2026 Nr. 268' -> /baymbl/2026-268/, PDF is per-document). Do not conflate.
RSS feeds are capped at 50 items with no paging — BayMBl publishes hundreds of docs/year, so poll at least weekly or you lose items; feeds carry documents, not issues.

No robots.txt, no sitemap, no JSON API, no OpenSearch, no ELI (verified 404s) — ingestion is RSS + HTML tables + static PDFs + the one CSV POST; server-rendered TYPO3 markup (data-label attributes on result tables are the most stable parse anchors but can change on relaunch).

RSS titles/descriptions can contain raw newlines inside the text (verified in gvbl feed item 'Vermessung und \nGeoinformation') and documents can carry multiple Gliederungsnummern (comma-separated) — normalize before keying.
Verify downloads against the published SHA-256 checksums (on detail pages and issue tables); note GVBl pages print them lowercase, BayMBl uppercase.

The /gvbl/ and /baymbl/ landing pages default to a recent-window result set — always pass explicit filters (volume/from/to) when enumerating, and paginate via ?offset=N (row-index based: offset=16 is page 2 at 15/page).
corrections_from_verify: JS-bundle claim 'zero fetch/XHR calls to JSON endpoints' is literally false: the bundle contains 2 fetch() calls, both from the TYPO3 femanager extension (POST to Femanager.getBaseUrl()+'index.php?id=...&type=1594138042' and a tx_femanager_validation[referrerAction] form POST). These are frontend-user form-validation endpoints, not a document/search API, so the substantive conclusion (search fully server-rendered, no JSON API for legal content) is confirmed — but a strict reader should know the bundle is not fetch-free.

BayMBl corpus size: claim says '~5,296 docs (last offset 5281)'; live /baymbl/ shows '5.283 Treffer' with the same last pagination offset 5281 — count off by ~13 (likely derived from offset arithmetic rather than the displayed total). Beware: Treffer counts use German thousands separators ('5.283'), so naive integer parsing reads 283.

GVBl search results table has no 'Typ' column (columns: Fundstelle/Verkündung/Titel/Gl-Nr./Ausfertigung), so the publicationType filter's effect is only verifiable via result titles — the filter itself works (see fragility notes).

N' to /baymbl/YYYY-N/ for full HTML text and /files/baymbl/YYYY/N/baymbl-YYYY-N.pdf. Practical stance: polite, attributed harvesting of search metadata for research plus PDF retrieval from origin Landtag servers is low-risk; wholesale republication of the abstract/Schlagwort layer is not clearly permitted. When in doubt email the Redaktion (they publish phone + email for exactly this).
tier: secondary — best-in-class cross-Länder discovery/monitoring index (all 16 Landtage, daily updates verified at 05.07.2026, normalized metadata, direct origin PDF links, stable GET permalinks), but HTML-scrape-only with 50/page cap, no feeds/export, metadata-not-fulltext search, and coverage only from ~1970; use it as the monitor/index layer and fetch authoritative full texts from the origin Landtag servers (which are the primary sources).

url_patterns: https://www.parlamentsspiegel.de/suche?query={TERM[+||+TERM2]}&type=vorgang&size={10|25|50}&page={N0}&detail=true — general search permalink (query supports '||' OR; weitern=true for wildcard)
https://www.parlamentsspiegel.de/suche?query={TERM}&qyHerk={BW|BAY|BLN|BRA|HB|HH|HES|MEVO|NDS|NW|RPF|SAL|SAC|SACA|SH|THUE}[&qyHerk=...] — restrict to one or more Länder (repeatable param)
https://www.parlamentsspiegel.de/suche?query={TERM}&qyVTyp={Anfrage|Antrag|Bericht|Beschlussempfehlung|Debatte|Gesetz|Sonstiges|Vorschrift|Wahl}[&qyVTyp=...] — Vorgangstyp filter (repeatable)
https://www.parlamentsspiegel.de/suche?query={TERM}&qyZeitAb={TT.MM.JJJJ|letzterMonat}&qyZeitBis={TT.MM.JJJJ|heute} — date window (symbolic values most reliable for recency)
https://www.parlamentsspiegel.de/suche?query={TERM}&qyVSys={code} — Sachgebiet facet (e.g. 5070='Ausländer, Migranten', 5100='Soziales'; 153 total)
https://www.parlamentsspiegel.de/suche?query={NN%2FNNNNN}&type=dokNr — lookup by Drucksachennummer (returns matches from ALL Länder with that number)

Origin PDF (per result href, host varies by Land, URL-encode spaces): https://www.bayern.landtag.de/www/ElanTextAblage_WP{P}/Drucksachen/{Kategorie}/{P}_{NNNNNNN}.pdf and 15 other Land-specific schemes
pitfalls: No machine-readable interface at all: no JSON/XML/CSV export, no OpenSearch description, no RSS/Atom (/rss 302s to a login page), sitemap.xml is 401, and Accept: application/json is ignored (returns text/html). HTML scraping of the GET permalinks is the only path.

Session handling: first request to /robots.txt, /parlamentsspiegel/suche etc. returns 302; use a cookie jar (JSESSIONID) and follow redirects; the canonical search endpoint is https://www.parlamentsspiegel.de/suche.
size parameter silently capped: 10/25/50 work, size=100 falls back to 10 without error — always verify by counting .ps-vorgang blocks; page is 0-based (page=0 is first page).

Origin PDF hrefs frequently contain unencoded spaces (e.g. '/Schriftliche Anfragen/19_0011106.pdf') — URL-encode before fetching; some origin links are plain http:// (lissh.lvn.parlanet.de, pardok.parlament-berlin.de etc.).

PDFs live on 16 different origin Landtag hosts, each with its own robots.txt, availability and URL scheme — fetch each host's robots.txt before harvesting (bayern.landtag.de verified OK for /www/ElanTextAblage_*).

Drucksachennummern collide across Länder (19/11106 exists in Niedersachsen, Bayern AND Berlin) — never key on DrsNr alone; use the portal's composite ID {LAND}_V{vorgang}_D{dokument} from the title attribute / collapse anchors.
Hit count and pagination must be parsed from the embedded 'window.jSuche = {cntHit: N, pgLast: M}' JS object — there is no clean DOM counter node.
Historical coverage starts ~1970 (0 hits 1947-1969, only 2106 'Gesetz' hits 1970-1989 vs 13547 in 2000-2009) — unusable for pre-1970 research; per-Land depth varies.

Explicit narrow recent date windows can return 0 hits while the symbolic values work (query=Asyl&qyZeitAb=22.06.2026 -> 0, but qyZeitAb=letzterMonat -> 3) — prefer symbolic letzterMonat/heute for monitoring and treat explicit-date results near the present with suspicion (indexing lag).
Search matches editorial metadata (Titel, Schlagwort, Zusammenfassung — <mark> highlights confirm), NOT the PDF full text — recall is limited by the indexers' Schlagwort choices.
Unknown paths 302-redirect to the search page or /parlamentsspiegel/login rather than 404ing — do not interpret a 302/200-after-redirect as evidence a probed resource exists.
Results HTML is large (~270 KB per 10-hit detail page) and Thymeleaf-generated with commented-out debris; parse defensively on class names (ps-vorgang, ps-titel, ps-dokument, ps-fundstelle).
corrections_from_verify: sitemap.xml claim says '401 (empty body)' — the body is actually a 439-byte Tomcat error page ('HTTP Status 401 – Unautorisiert', text/html). Status and dead-end conclusion are correct; only the snippet is inaccurate.
CSP snippet is abbreviated: actual connect-src is "'self' *.nrw.de svc.webspellchecker.net" (webspellchecker host omitted). Irrelevant to server-side harvesting as the claim itself notes.

bayern.landtag.de robots.txt additionally fully blocks named bots (sitebot, Baiduspider, Ahrefsbot, jobs.de-Robot, MJ12bot) — not mentioned in the claim; a generic research UA is unaffected, User-agent:* section matches the claim verbatim and allows ElanTextAblage paths.

Form contains 189 qyVSys checkboxes, not 153; the '153 Sachgebiete' figure is the site's own statistic (hierarchical parent/child codes explain the difference). Claimed example codes verified: 5070='Ausländer, Migranten', 5100='Soziales'.
corpus_hints: For the SNTIQ asylum/social-practice corpus: (1) Daily monitor query: GET /suche?query=Asyl+%7C%7C+Aufnahmegesetz+%7C%7C+Fl%C3%BCchtling&qyZeitAb=letzterMonat&qyZeitBis=heute&type=vorgang&size=50&detail=true — query syntax supports '||' (OR); symbolic dates 'letzterMonat'/'heute' are more reliable than explicit narrow date windows. (2) Restrict by Land with repeatable qyHerk= (BW, BAY, BLN, BRA, HB, HH, HES, MEVO, NDS, NW, RPF, SAL, SAC, SACA, SH, THUE). (3) Vorgangstyp filter qyVTyp= (Anfrage, Antrag, Bericht, Beschlussempfehlung, Debatte, Gesetz, Sonstiges, Vorschrift, Wahl) — use qyVTyp=Gesetz + query=Aufnahmegesetz to track Land-Aufnahmegesetz legislative processes; Kleine Anfragen (fqDTyp=KlAnfr) are the richest source on administrative asylum practice. (4) Sachgebiet facet: qyVSys=5070 = 'Ausländer, Migranten', qyVSys=5100 = 'Soziales' (153 Sachgebiete total; no dedicated 'Asyl' Sachgebiet — Asyl appears as Schlagwort). (5) Parse window.jSuche {cntHit, pgLast} from each page for pagination; walk page=0..pgLast with size=50. (6) Always keep the portal's composite ID ({LAND}_V{vorgang}_D{dokument}) as primary key — Drucksachennummern collide across Länder (19/11106 exists in NDS, BAY and BLN). (7) Fetch full texts as PDFs from the 16 origin Landtag servers via the direct hrefs; treat Parlamentsspiegel as index/monitor only. Search matches Titel/Schlagwort/Abstrakt metadata, not PDF full text.

verify_verdict: SOLID — all 11 claimed endpoints reproduced this session with exactly the claimed HTTP statuses, content types, and content, down to suspicious-looking specifics that would expose fabrication (SPARQL counts 123 and 162, empty DEU bindings, 69963-byte RSS with CELEX:32026R1507 as first item, 132227-byte OpenAPI spec with the 500-req/5-min sentence, 585-byte OEIL JSON 404 body, verbatim MNE celex/title/ojnum/ojdate). No hidden auth, API keys, or omitted mandatory params anywhere. All four robots.t
robots: publications.europa.eu/robots.txt: 301 -> op.europa.eu/robots.txt -> 403 Azure WAF JS challenge; robots not retrievable, SPARQL endpoint itself unrestricted and answers 200. eur-lex.europa.eu/robots.txt: HTTP 202, empty body, header x-amzn-waf-action: challenge (AWS WAF via CloudFront) — robots not retrievable; the WAF challenges all HTML paths regardless of User-Agent, but exempts the /EN/display-feed.rss servlet. data.europarl.europa.eu/robots.txt: 200, 'User-agent: * / Allow: /', Sitemap: https://data.europarl.europa.eu/sitemap.xml — everything allowed; API spec states rate limit 500 requests/5 min. oeil.secure.europarl.europa.eu/robots.txt: host redirects to oeil.europarl.europa.eu, robots.txt 404 (no restrictions published). No Crawl-delay found anywhere; self-imposed 1 req/s used.
licensing: EU open data reuse regime confirmed applicable: EUR-Lex legal notice states the reuse policy is based on Commission Decision 2011/833/EU — legal documents reusable for commercial and non-commercial purposes with source acknowledgment (verified via archived legal notice; live page WAF-blocked; noted exceptions e.g. International Accounting Standards). EU primary/secondary legislation texts are official works. EP Open Data API declares CC BY 4.0 in its own OpenAPI spec (verified live). German implementing acts referenced by MNE records are amtliche Werke under § 5 UrhG at national level; CELLAR only stores their bibliographic citation, not text.
tier: primary — for CELLAR SPARQL (auth-free, complete directive+MNE graph incl. German citations) and the EP Open Data API (CC BY 4.0 JSON-LD procedures/adopted texts); EUR-Lex website itself only via the RSS servlet (rssId=222 for daily OJ L) since all HTML is WAF-challenged; OEIL seed_only (stable human-facing procedure URLs, no machine interface).

Wrong CDM property fails SILENTLY: cdm:resource_legal_implements_resource_legal returns empty bindings with HTTP 200; the correct property is cdm:measure_national_implementing_implements_resource_legal — validate queries against a directive with known transpositions (32019L0790) before trusting empty results
Empty German MNE set can be a legitimate state, not a bug: 32024L1385 has 162 MNEs EU-wide but 0 from Germany (deadline 2027) as of 2026-07-06
MNE records contain only bibliographic citations of German acts (title, BGBl number/date/page) — no act text; resolve text via German sources (BGBl) using the citation

publications.europa.eu/robots.txt redirects to op.europa.eu which is 403 Azure-WAF-blocked — robots policy unknowable; keep voluntary 1 req/s throttling on the SPARQL endpoint, and expect slow/timeout on heavy unscoped queries (Virtuoso)

EUR-Lex predefined rssId values are only documented on a WAF-blocked help page (recovered via Wayback: 161,162,163,164,221,222); invalid ids return HTTP 200 with a 'feed doesn't exist' XML stub — validate feed body, not status code
EP API: unfiltered /procedures times out (>30 s); always fetch by process-id or filter; rate limit 500 req/5 min; the format parameter value must be URL-encoded (application%2Fld%2Bjson)

OEIL legacy machine interfaces are gone: ficheprocedure.rss 404, /oeil/en/rss 404, search export.do silently redirects to homepage; oeil.secure.europarl.europa.eu redirects to oeil.europarl.europa.eu (update stored URLs)

url_patterns: PDF: https://www.bundesrat.de/SharedDocs/drucksachen/<YYYY>/<RRRR>-<RRRR+99>/<nr>-<yy>.pdf?__blob=publicationFile&v=1  — YYYY = 2000+yy (from the /yy suffix of the Drucksachennummer); range dir = zero-padded hundred block containing nr: lower = floor((nr-1)/100)*100+1, upper = lower+99, both %04d (296/25 -> 2025/0201-0300/296-25.pdf; 173/24 -> 2024/0101-0200/173-24.pdf; 763/25 -> 2025/0701-0800/763-25.pdf). All 5 test numbers verified 200.
Beschluss: same path with '(B)' appended before .pdf, literal unencoded parens: .../2024/0101-0200/173-24(B).pdf?__blob=publicationFile&v=1 (verified 200, 15 pages, full adopted bill text).

Sub-numbered Drucksachen (Ausschussempfehlungen etc.): <nr>-<sub>-<yy> in the base-nr range dir, e.g. .../2026/0301-0400/388-1-26.html (observed in RSS; corresponding .pdf?__blob=publicationFile expected).
Detail page (HTML metadata): same path with .html instead of .pdf (robots-allowed; RSS items link here).
Feeds: https://www.bundesrat.de/SiteGlobals/Functions/RSSFeed/RSSGenerator_{PBPrintout|Announcement|Publication|Event|Event_Ausschuss|top_plenumkompakt}.xml
pitfalls: HEAD requests are rejected (303 -> /error_path/400.html) by the Airlock WAF — always GET, and treat 303-to-error_path as 'blocked request shape', not 'document missing'.
The .pdf URL WITHOUT ?__blob=publicationFile returns HTTP 200 text/html (the detail page) — a naive downloader saves HTML as .pdf. Always require Content-Type: application/pdf.
robots Crawl-delay is 30 seconds — a full-year backfill (~800 Drucksachen + variants) takes ~7h/year at compliant speed; plan slow backfill, use RSS (ttl 60 min) for increments.
RSS feeds live under robots-Disallowed /SiteGlobals/Functions/ despite being advertised for subscription — document the compliance stance; poll at most hourly.

Wrong-tenant responses observed: one 200 response for the bundesrat.de Impressum URL was actually a Bayerischer Landtag TYPO3 page (shared hosting/LB misroute). Validate every response (expected host in canonical link / 'Bundesrat' marker) before ingesting.
Transient TLS failure on first contact (curl exit 35); retry with backoff.

Not every Drucksache contains the bill text: 296/25 is a 1-page transmittal letter (Gesetzesantrag re-introduction); the substantive text sits in the referenced earlier Drucksache or the (B) version. Check page count / doc type (Gesetzesantrag vs Gesetzentwurf vs Beschluss).

pdftotext artifacts: spaced-out header ('Bun d esrat') and vertical sidebar strings ('R Fu ss' = 'Fristablauf'-style margin marks) interleave with body text — strip first-page header region or filter short-token lines.

No sitemap (404), no directory listing (302), search form is robots-disallowed GSB HTML — discovery is numeric iteration (1..~1000 per year + sub-numbers + (B)) plus the two RSS feeds; expect 404-shaped GSB error pages (HTML, sometimes 200-ish renders) for gaps.
The v= query version varies per document on the site (v=1 worked for all 5 tests, but treat it as optional/ignored rather than semantic).
